original text:
Right now Utahns who earn between 100-400% of the federal poverty level are eligible to receive premium subsidies to purchase private insurance on healthcare.gov.
However, individuals who earn below 100% FPL - even $100 below that limit - make too little to use healthcare.gov.
A single person earning less than $11,490 and a family of three earning less than $19,530 can't receive subsidies.
Plus, many of them earn too much to qualify for Utah Medicaid.
They are teenage cancer patients.
They are residents of every legislative district in Utah.
And they are realizing that Medicaid expansion would help then live healthier and more productive lives.
Here are two stories from Utah's Coverage Gap.
Ryan was an associate provost at a Utah college.
He has a Ph.D. and over 30 years of experience in higher education administration.
But when Ryan lost his job in December, he found himself, his wife, and son without affordable health insurance coverage options.
Ryan knew he had to seek assistance immediately as his wife has diabetes and needs medications that cost over $1,000 a month.
When he applied for coverage on healthcare.gov he was told he qualified for Medicaid.
But when he applied for Medicaid through the Utah Department of Workforce Services, he was told he didn't qualify in Utah.
Ryan and his family are caught in Utah's coverage gap.
Because he is currently unemployed, and his wife is unable to work due to ongoing medical conditions, they don't qualify for Medicaid.
If Utah expands Medicaid, Ryan and his wife would have health insurance while he searches for a new job.
For Ryan, health insurance means stabilizing his wife's illness and avoiding the threat of medical bankruptcy.
David was laid off in November, 2013.
He and his wife JoAnn knew that they needed insurance, so they purchased COBRA insurance.
Their cost for one month of COBRA coverage was $800.
Realizing they couldn't extend coverage beyond the one month, they applied for health insurance on healthcare.gov.
They were told they qualified for Medicaid in Utah.
When they applied for Medicaid, they were told they don't meet categorical requirements to be eligible.
JoAnn has Charcot Foot, a common problem for diabetics.
Both JoAnn and David are hopeful that he will get a new job with health insurance.
Utah's numbers were included in the 29-page data dump.
>>A total of 6,186 Utahns have finished applications (some for more than one person), but have not yet picked a plan.
>>3,883 of the Utah applicants qualified for premium subsidies to make their insurance premiums more affordable.
>>Successful Medicaid and CHIP applicants accounted for 4,816 Utahns on the marketplace.
An unknown number also applied through state agencies.
>>Utahns are waiting until the December 15 and March 31 deadlines to purchase their marketplace insurance.
Here's a comment from the 11/13/13 Salt Lake Tribune article, Fewer than 400 Utahns able to shop Obamacare exchange.
>>How many people do you know have purchased all of their Christmas presents?
Face it: We are a last-minute society.
Failed Launch or Slow Start?
-If I create a new login will it work?
-How about applying at 2am?
No, that's when the website is being overhauled.
Good thing pre-existing conditions don't matter anymore.
Two weeks after the debut of the Affordable Care Act's marketplaces - including healthcare.gov here in Utah-the promise of reform has been temporarily replaced by an error message.
But it will get fixed.
Yes, some Utahns have signed up for new insurance.
If it wasn't for the success of Phil Sherburne, Leia Bell and their three kids, we'd all probably be at stage four: "Depression."
Soon after October 1st Phil signed up his family for a silver-level insurance policy that cost them $123 a month.
And it took him "about an hour," he told the Salt Lake Tribune.
How?
We don't know.
As we drafted this article our healthcare.gov login screen was stuck in digital purgatory.
Criticism of healthcare.gov's abortive launch is blunt and universal.
Ezra Klein called the debut a "failure" in the Washington Post's Wonkblog.
The New York Times quoted an insurance official describing the federal website as "awful, just awful."
And Avik Roy, never a fan of Obamacare, headlined a recent a Forbes.com post with "third-world experience," referring to a now prescient March 2013 warning from the chief information officer at the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS).
Still, the middle of October is 14 days into a 180-day open enrollment period.
Even if you count February 15th as the drop-dead enrollment date to avoid a tax penalty, about 90 percent of the enrollment window remains.
And so the question isn't whether healthcare.gov will be fixed.
The question is... when it is finally fixed; will the millions of people who have struggled to log on notice that the website's door is now open?
Perhaps yes, suggests this opinion piece in the Times.
"Interested customers will come back often," it explains, citing an average of 18 inquiries on the 2006-era Massachusetts Health Connector website before a person signed up for coverage.
The Obama administration has set a goal of seven million Americans signed up for new coverage in 2014.
And for the marketplaces to maintain the proper risk balance, many of the new enrollees must be the "young invincibles" for whom health insurance isn't a medical necessity, but convenient financial security or something that makes mom happy.
So far the state-run exchanges are doing better than the federal exchanges - including 1,000 applicants a day in Kentucky, and 305 newly covered lives during the first week of Colorado's marketplace.
Where is Utah?
Besides the Sherburne-Bell family - we don't know.
This Advisory Board post estimates 5,000 successful applicants among the 26 states with federal marketplaces - compared to almost 25,000 in the state-run exchanges.
As of mid-October, Utah data is non-existent.
Plus, we can promote helpful workarounds like the Kaiser Family Foundation's subsidy calculator - and the new plan preview tool at healthcare.gov - which doesn't require a login or identity verification.
Plus, UHPP's calendar of "Health Reform 101" presentations is staying active.
We've held 67 events since January, reaching almost 2,900 Utahns.
And we have 15 more educational events scheduled for the second half of October, including presentations in Provo, Orem, Ogden, and Moab.
To schedule a "just the facts" educational presentation about the ACA for your group or organization, email Jason Stevenson, or call him 801-433-2299, x5.
Utah's decision on whether to expand Medicaid coverage is one of the last pieces of the health reform puzzle to fall into place.
And thanks to recent comments and events, we now have a likely timeline for when a decision will be made.
A community workgroup, organized by the Utah Department of Health, has been studying Medicaid expansion throughout the summer.
To cover more ground, the workgroup split into five subgroups each focused on a specific approach: Full expansion; Partial expansion; Block grant; Full-family coverage; and Charity care.
UHPP has been active in all subgroups, and Matt Slonaker co-chairs the Full expansion group.
This recent Salt Lake Tribune article describes the self-reported pluses and minuses of each approach to expansion.
Next up: After the full community workgroup meets again on September 5th, each subgroup will report during Governor Gary Herbert's Health Summit on September 26th.
Then Gov.
Hebert will decide in December whether to include funding for Medicaid expansion in his next budget proposal.
If he proposes some kind of Medicaid expansion, the governor still needs the approval of the Utah legislature during the 2014 legislative session.
According to a state-sponsored study, 123,000 Utahns (including these folks) would become eligible for coverage if Utah expands Medicaid.
On August 1st the community workgroup met to hear to preliminary reports from each of the five subgroups.
This event also served as a dress rehearsal for the governor's health summit in September.
UHPP observed two major developments during the August 1st meeting.
First, Dr. Sven Wilson, a BYU economist who earned a PhD at the University of Chicago, told the workgroup that Medicaid expansion is a good, lean investment for the state.
Dr. Wilson also shared his calculation that expanding Medicaid would cost Utah around $158 per new enrollee per year (see page 4).
Second, consensus is building across multiple subgroups to employ premium subsidies as an alternative to the traditional Medicaid expansion (see more below).
From UHPP's perspective, the August 1st meeting was productive and educational for the workgroup members and the general public.
However, one idea did not receive enough attention: The notion that federal government will only fund a plan that covers Utah's entire Medicaid expansion population.
Most of the August 1st meeting was taken up with presentations by the five subgroups.
Each subgroup had at least one plan and the Block Grant group, headed by Sen. Todd Weiler (R-Woods Cross) discussed three proposals.
Some presentations were substantive, and others were more conceptual.
But one thing was evident after hearing from all the groups: Progress is being made as Utah's policymakers and lawmakers focus on studying the issue and developing real solutions.
One theme shared by several subgroups was the use of premium subsidies to enable low-income Utahns to purchase private insurance instead of get Medicaid.
This approach would leverage new federal funds to help Medicaid beneficiaries purchase private coverage through a state's new health insurance exchanges.
Arkansas pioneered this approach, and states like Ohio, Tennessee, Florida, and Pennsylvania might follow its lead.
Three subgroups (Full expansion, Partial expansion, and Block Grant) are considering subsidy-based alternatives to the Medicaid expansion.
The good news is that, if done right, a subsidy-based plan may be eligible for the federal match that starts at 100% and phases down to 90% by 2020.
Unfortunately, in UHPP's estimation, not enough attention was paid to whether the various premium-subsidy proposals would be eligible for the 100% to 90% federal match.
Without the full match, Utah would be stuck with the same finite resources of the current 70% federal match rate.
Plus, the federal government has made it clear that partial expansion plans won't receive the full match rate.
Going forward, UHPP will work to determine whether Utah's various premium-subsidy proposals will be eligible for the full federal match.
Several meetings in August and September will lay the groundwork for the state's upcoming decision on Medicaid expansion.
Note: During this meeting the committee will discuss the model for charity care outlined in the 8/16/13 document "Strengthening Our Safety Net: A Proposal."
This proposal would recommend that Utah charter a nonprofit to establish eight outpatient clinics along the Wasatch Front where patients could receive free care.
Three clinics would be located in Salt Lake county, two in Utah county, and one each in Davis, Weber, and Cache counties.
According to recent remarks by Gov.
Herbert, his decision on the Medicaid expansion will be reflected in his budget proposal in December.
Last Wednesday HHS released the final rules on Essential Health Benefits (EHB) - the "floor" of ten benefits (like hospitalization, maternity and newborn care, and mental health services) that must be offered by insurance plans on each state's individual and small business exchanges, Medicaid state plans, and on any new insurance plans offered outside the exchanges.
The rule also prohibits discrimination based on age or pre-existing conditions, describes prescription drug benefits and determines levels of coverage.
For more details on what changed between the proposed rules unveiled last November and the update last week, check out this excellent summary from Georgetown's Center for Children and Families.
Utah did not change or upgrade its EHB standard during the comment period.
See Utah's current EHB standard here: As you can see, there are not a lot of frills in Utah's standard, though the state seems to meet the minimum requirements.
For example, that standard still covers only eight outpatient mental health visits per year.
But Nehring says the tacit approval given by HHS to Utah's benchmark plan seems to indicate that they either didn't notice that Utah's age limitations for children's dental and vision care in violation of the Affordable Care Act standards, or they didn't care.
Next Step: UHPP and partners will take up these issues directly with CCIIO (the agency that oversees exchanges) in our conference call with the head of CCIIO, Gary Cohen, and his staff on February 27th.
>>Check out the final rule on Essential Health Benefits.

original text:
Does a Baroque bow need to be more tensioned than a modern bow?
Not really sure about boroque bow tension, but I would think you would need enough to make it playable like a modern bow and be able to adjust the tension with the screw, (if it has one), I'm unable to find the pic you posted of it.
As far as hair width, a hank of bow hair usuall contain's 150-200 hair's.
If the hair is narrower at the frog, there is a possibility that the wooden shim/spreader has come out between the hair and the ferrule.
This happened with my pernambuco bow I had rehaired last year.
I noticed that the hair was very narrow at the frog, but normal width at the tip, I knew I hadn't lost any hair's, so when I took it to another luthier, he said it hadn't been rehaired properly and that the wood shim/spreader had fallen out letting the hair's clump together, they were only about 7 mm wide.
After it was fixed, the hair's now measure 11mm at the frog.
Incidentally, you really don't realize how fine the hair's are untill you start counting them.
One of my bow's with "Fiddlershop" hair counted 178 hair's.
Definately not good on the eye's.
No, it's the same thickness all the way.
It looks as if it's as it should be.
And yes, it does have a screw.
They have quite an odd bow.
They are also tuned in fifths like a violin.
They look like a violin that has been in a bad car accident and when reassembled, they didn't have all the parts.
One great thing about the Erhu, is that the bow never get's seperated or lost from the instrument itself.
I hope you have a wonderfull time in China, I have a friend in, Taiyuan, city, Shanxi Province.
Be safe, John and good.
Not sure about the required tension either.
The whole thing about bow tension in general is to find the tension that is not so loose that the bow stick touches and scrapes the strings too much when playing loudly and not so tight that the bow bounces uncontrollably.
In other words, you'll need to guess at first and experiment and find the best compromise.
hmm...since the cambering in the bow is the same as a modern bow, it should be the same as the tension of the modern bow, as long as it is not touching the stick and it's easy to do techniques, but it has a snakewood stick, so it's harder than the more flexible pernambuco........ erhus are awesome!!!!
They look like a violin that has been in a bad car accident and when reassembled, they didn't have all the parts.
yes, John, did not have all the parts so they substitute with something else handy beside the fact that somebody stole 2 strings and leaving only two strings left.
Your future Erhu may have its "top plate" made with snake skin, python skin, perhaps, lol.
Well, there were sheep guts and horse hairs for fiddles, and snake skin is also organic, right?
Have a safe trip and enjoy your time there, John.

SAN JOSE  -  Cityview Plaza, a huge office, restaurant and retail complex in downtown San Jose, has been bought in a $284 million mega deal that highlights robust investor interest in the Bay Area's largest city  -  and appears to mark the biggest commercial property sale in Silicon Valley so far in 2018.
The deal comes amid a flurry of property sales and development proposals in nearby areas of downtown San Jose.
The complex is a few blocks away from Diridon Station and the SAP Center, where Google plans a massive, transit-oriented complex of offices, restaurants, shops, residences, open spaces and other amenities in a development where 15,000 to 20,000 of the search giant's employees would eventually work.
It's also across the street from the three-building Adobe Systems headquarters, which Adobe intends to expand with a fourth office tower.
SJ Cityview, an affiliate of San Francisco-based developer Jay Paul, purchased the 580,000-square-foot complex, according to Santa Clara County property records that were filed July 23.
The seller was Park Center Plaza Investors, an affiliate of realty investment firm Equus Capital Partners.
The Jay Paul affiliate paid $283.5 million in cash for the property, which is bounded by West San Fernando Street, South Almaden Boulevard, Park Avenue and South Market Street, county documents show.
The complex consists of 534,000 square feet of offices and 46,000 square feet of restaurant and retail space.
"The mixed-use Class A campus consists of high-rise and mid-rise office buildings, retail and restaurant space, united by a newly designed plaza area with outdoor seating, artwork and water features," according to an Equus web page about Cityview Plaza.
Along with several large office buildings, Cityview Plaza contains 40,000 square feet of restaurant and retail spaces, including Specialty's Cafe & Bakery, Morton's Steakhouse, Scott's Seafood, Peggy Sue's, Bank of America, Heritage Bank and Wells Fargo Bank.
In recent years, Equus launched a wide-ranging renovation that added a new fitness center, conference center, a fountain and upgraded landscaping, a property brochure stated.
Colliers International and Cushman & Wakefield, commercial realty brokerages marketing the complex, stated in a brochure that Cityview Plaza commands a top-notch site.
The property is perched near the Fairmont Hotel and the Paseo de San Antonio.
"Cityview Plaza is centrally located in downtown San Jose" and features an "amenity-rich environment," the brochure stated.
The Cityview Plaza deal, at $283.5 million, looks to be the biggest commercial real estate acquisition so far during 2018 in Santa Clara County, as indicated by a review of major property purchases in the South Bay reported by this news organization.
In January, a 64,000-square-foot Palo Alto office building occupied by Visa was bought for $138 million, notable for its head-spinning price of $2,165 a square foot.
In March, a big office building in Mountain View that's leased to Google, along with adjacent land suitable for development, was bought for $169.9 million.
Steven Golubchik, Edmund Najera and David Hosler, executives with realty brokerage NKF Capital Markets, arranged the purchase of Cityview Plaza.
"With one of the largest Caltrain stations and a BART line under development, downtown San Jose will be one of the most easily accessible and interconnected locations in Northern California, making this a strategic investment," said Golubchik, a vice chairman with NKF Capital Markets.

Deposition Author(s): Loll, B., Kern, J., Saenger, W., Zouni, A., Biesiadka, J.
Loll, B., Kern, J., Saenger, W., Zouni, A., Biesiadka, J.
Oxygenic photosynthesis in plants, algae and cyanobacteria is initiated at photosystem II, a homodimeric multisubunit protein-cofactor complex embedded in the thylakoid membrane.
Photosystem II captures sunlight and powers the unique photo-induced oxidation of water to atmospheric oxygen.
Crystallographic investigations of cyanobacterial photosystem II have provided several medium-resolution structures (3.8 to 3.2 A) that explain the general arrangement of the protein matrix and cofactors, but do not give a full picture of the complex.
Here we describe the most complete cyanobacterial photosystem II structure obtained so far, showing locations of and interactions between 20 protein subunits and 77 cofactors per monomer.
Assignment of 11 beta-carotenes yields insights into electron and energy transfer and photo-protection mechanisms in the reaction centre and antenna subunits.
The high number of 14 integrally bound lipids reflects the structural and functional importance of these molecules for flexibility within and assembly of photosystem II.
A lipophilic pathway is proposed for the diffusion of secondary plastoquinone that transfers redox equivalents from photosystem II to the photosynthetic chain.
The structure provides information about the Mn4Ca cluster, where oxidation of water takes place.
Our study uncovers near-atomic details necessary to understand the processes that convert light to chemical energy.
Institut für Chemie und Biochemie/Kristallographie, Freie Universität Berlin, Takustrasse 6, D-14195 Berlin, Germany.

By Alexandra Bruell .
Published on September 29, 2015.
The current programmatic space is wrought with challenges, but programmatic 3.0, as an Advertising Week panel coined it, pales in comparison to the challenges of programmatic 2.0.
Programmatic 1.0 arose from a need to sell remnant inventory, explained Shenan Reed, president of digital for WPP's MEC in North America.
"There was better ROI for us [in 2.0], but it led to a lot of pushing down on price and dilution of quality," she said.
"[Programmatic] 1.0 had better quality, but 2.0 was wrought with challenges.
3.0 is our turn to say we want to make it work harder for us."
In the early days of programmatic, when publishers were trying to sell extra inventory, sell rates were only 10% to 30%, she said.
"The challenge was in creating revenue that was meaningful.
They ultimately sold off too much inventory at too low a price."
Today, it's about getting back to quality inventory that reaches the right people with the right messaging, agreed panelists.
"There was a time we had to sacrifice scale for quality.
Now big publishers like me don't need to do that anymore," said Jeremy Randol, VP of programmatic sales strategy at Pandora.
"2016 will be the year of programmatic mobile.
We've seen companies make strides in defining cross-device [metrics].
DSPs are doing a better job.
And there's a migration toward more quality connections."
He said Pandora one and a half years ago hired people who could make programmatic a mainstream sales tactic.
His team has grown to 10 people.
Ms. Reed asked the group if in the next version of the marketplace advertisers will be able to buy against a cost-per-acquisition metric.
Currently, most buying happens on a CPM, or cost-per-thousand-impression metric.
"We'll sell against how effective the advertising is," said Mark Zagorski, CEO of Nielsen's Exelate.
"We're moving into a world where everything is sold on a CPA basis."
"We'll get to a place where sellers can make some real money again," added Ms. Reed.
"We've been pushing media partners down and down on these CPMs because we don't see it working.
If we can get to the conversion we want, and the metric of success we want, I'm willing to pay more for that success metric."

Every month we get together with a few of our favorite neighbors for Taco Night.
We take turns hosting and stuff our faces with homemade tacos, chips, salsa, and play card games until late in the night.
Taco Nights are so fun because everyone brings a dish to share.
One neighbor always brings a different variety of gourmet salsas for us to taste-test.
Another brings a collection of hot sauces!
No matter what though, everyone always asks me to bring this guacamole.
I have a favorite guacamole recipe I have been perfecting for the past few years.
Pulse a few times until coarsely chopped.
You'll be adding a few more things and processing it more, so it doesn't have to be too finely diced at this stage.
Next, chop off the top of a jalapeno and split in half.
If you can handle the heat, go ahead and throw it in just like this.
As well as a handful (about a cup) of cilantro.
Just roughly chop the leaves off the stems and throw them in the bowl.
Pulse a few more times until coarsely chopped.
Next, grab two plum (aka roma) tomatoes and split in half.
You could stop here and have pico de gallo!
When choosing avocados to make guacamole, they should give when squeezed, but not be mushy.
And PLENTY of salt and pepper.
I usually start with 1/2 teaspoon each.
And for that "secret ingredient" flavor, add a dash of cumin.
Finally, add as much of the pico de gallo mixture to the avocados as you'd like to get your desired texture.
Stir with a fork until just combined.
I like to leave my guacamole a little chunky.
I usually have about 1/2 to 1 cup of the pico mixture leftover.
Place the rest of the pico de gallo in separate bowl for dipping chips and topping on tacos.
To serve, place the guacamole in a bowl and cover with slices of lime.
This will keep the guacamole from turning brown!
As the night goes on and the guacamole gets eaten, people can remove slices of lime to reveal more fresh guacamole.
Peel and quarter a red onion and place in the bowl of a food processor.
Pulse a few times until coarsely chopped.
Remove the seeds from a jalapeno, then cut into 4 pieces and add to bowl.
Add the cilantro and pulse a few more times until coarsely chopped.
Cut the tomatoes in half, remove the seeds, and add to the bowl.
Pulse a few more times until finely chopped.
Cut the avocados in half, remove the pits, and scoop out the flesh into a separate bowl.
Add the lemon and lime juice, salt, pepper, and cumin if desired.
Add all but one cup of the onion mixture to the avocados and stir with a fork until just combined.
I prefer to leave my guacamole a little chunky.
Add more of the onion mixture if desired.
I usually use all but 1/2-1 cup of the mixture.
Set out the remaining onion mixture (pico de gallo) for dipping chips or topping on tacos.

I love guacamole!
I could eat it every day, but I find most recipes are bland.
This one looks like it would have all the flavour I need.
Really looking forward to trying it; love the idea of adding a little cumin.
great tip on the lime slices.
Just wanted to confirm you use a whole red onion, not 1/2?
Your ingredients state 1/2 red onion.
It's like two recipes in one shot!
Pico and guac :) Great tip at the end!
I've heard of adding lime juice to keep avocados from turning brown, but never covering with lime slices...mind blown!
What a fun tradition to have with your neighbors!
Looks delicious!
Not to mention that avocados are an incredibly healthy near-perfect food loaded with important nutrients, such as potassium.
I love to see mothers giving their little ones avocados straight out of the skin!
Great to something so nutritious among your recipes!
Sounds nice Kevin and Amanda.
Thank you and have a great week!
Guacamole is one of our favorite things  -  I can't wait to try this recipe!
LOVE that trick with the limes!!
GENIUS!

original text:
It's surprisingly easy to create and print replacement parts.
In this hands on, David Gewirtz shows how he created a remote control cover replacement.
The big picture: 3D printing enables virtual spare parts inventories.
The big picture: 3D printing enables virtual spare parts inventories.
3D printing is often touted as a great tool for designers to test and refine their designs prior to manufacturing.
But you can do a lot more with 3D printing, even with inexpensive home printers.
For example, you can use a 3D printer to create and produce a replacement part for something you use every day.
In my case, I made a replacement back cover for a remote control.
I have a desk fan I use all the time, the Vornado Flippi V Compact Air Circulator.
It lives a few feet away from me, under my desk.
It's small, powerful, and quiet.
What makes it particularly useful is a remote control I can use to adjust the speed and oscillation, without ever moving away from my keyboard.
This is great because I can turn it on and off when on the phone, or just tap the remote control when I'm too busy or lazy to move the five feet from my chair to the fan.
The problem is that I drop the remote all the time, and the back cover for the battery compartment tends to fly off and get lost.
It really interrupts workflow to have to crawl around under my desk to look for the tiny part.
Obviously, whatever you need to make will be different from what I worked on.
The key point is that a 3D printer can be a great resource for useful projects like crafting replacement parts for objects you use.
Lots of people lose a part or two from a beloved object.
After dropping the remote for probably the 20th time, and not being able to locate the cover to the battery compartment after a long search, I decided enough was enough.
Has anyone seen my missing cover?
It's probably still somewhere down there, under my desk, unless the elves have taken it.
I decided to make a new one.
I'm fortunate, because I have a number of 3D printers in the shop to choose from.
If I weren't writing about 3D printing, I'd probably only have one.
For this project, I chose to use the most inexpensive of them, the $399 New Matter MOD-t.
The MOD-t isn't as fast as the LulzBot or the MakerBot, nor does it create objects of super high quality, but it's a fine little printer.
I was curious about whether I could use it for this purpose.
The cover is quite small, making it possible to do a lot of print attempts.
Each print only took about 20 minutes.
Because I spent just a few minutes each time refining the design, the print time for each "draft" didn't bother me at all.
The key to the design was exploring and understanding the geometry of the cover.
That may sound difficult, but all it really takes is observation, measuring, a little creativity, and the willingness to test.
The basic shape is that of a rectangle with a half-circle at the end.
So I created a rectangle and circle in 123D Design and placed the circle over the rectangle.
I then shrunk the thickness to 1.5 millimeters.
To get the width and length of the cover space, I used a pair of $29.95 digital calipers I picked up from Harbor Freight.
Calipers are key, because they allow you to measure the distances for elements you need to make.
You can usually get measurements down to a tenth of a millimeter, which is pretty precise.
Of course, my measurements were always off by a bit, so once I designed the object based on my measurements, I had to print it, and then tweak the print to get things to fit.
First, I printed the basic shape, the rectangle and circle.
You can see how this is made up of two simple shapes: a rectangle and a circle.
Then I added a tab, so that it would fit in the slot in the remote.
This took a few tries, because I had to get the right size, but also get the raised part to fit just so.
It also wasn't hard.
It just took the patience to tinker with it.
Next, I added the back hook.
If you look at the image below, you can see the slot on the remote control body, and the hook I created.
That, too, took a few iterations until I got it to fit right.
This is made with just two small rectangles at right angles.
Next, I added the back tail of the cover, again with some level of iteration.
This becomes the outside of the cover.
Finally, it all fit and closed over the remote.
The only problem was, it didn't work.
This was when I was reasonably convinced this whole project was a very bad idea.
But it's also here that persistence was important.
I spent some time examining the remote.
I tested the battery with the remote and no cover, and found that by holding the battery just so, it would fire the remote.
This meant that the cover replacement I was making would not require any wiring to complete a circuit.
It also told me that the changes I needed to make were really about how to hold the battery in the right place.
I added a flat circle spacer to push the battery down, and I also added another backing bar which pushed the battery forward into its contacts.
This, as it turns out, was the winning design.
It did work, but not regularly.
All that took was a few more iterations, eventually moving the back bar back just a tiny bit so the pressure was just right.
Then it worked.
Here's the final piece.
As you can see, it's not much larger than a penny.
Here's the final part.
It's not much bigger than a penny.
I learned quite a few lessons in building this part, some of which may be valuable to you.
First, let me reinforce that before I started learning about 3D printing about a year ago, I did not do mechanical constructions of any type.
Yes, I have an engineering degree, but it's in computer science.
I can design a circuit board.
I can design a computer language and an operating system.
Creating a tiny replacement part for a fan?
Before this year, you might as well have asked me to build a rocket in my backyard to travel to Mars.
Not in my wheelhouse.
Now, however, I've learned that the process of creating objects like this is really a matter of observation, measurement, and patience, as I mentioned above.
Once I mastered the basic skills of 123D Design (which, quite honestly, isn't much harder than PowerPoint or any other drawing program), I was on my way.
If you can draw a rectangle, you can do basic 3D design.
I also learned that an inexpensive 3D printer can get the job done.
It's not quite as clean an object as might be produced with a much more expensive printer, but it's good enough to do the job.
I cleaned off some of the plastic detritus with a file and a pair of clippers.
It works, which is all I really needed.
Finally, I learned that it's easy to do this over about two weeks time.
In actual elapsed time, this project probably took me about four hands-on hours (not counting waiting for the printer).
If I had to spend a weekend doing nothing by designing, printing, trying, tweaking, and doing it all over again, I would have gone completely around the bend.
But because I put in just a few minutes when I felt like it, the project proceeded easily.
It really was quite fun to do.
In addition to actually having my remote working again, there's a very interesting benefit to 3D printing a replacement part.
I now have a virtual inventory.
The 3D design model is now on my file server.
If I lose the cover again (and I most certainly will), I can just load the file on my printer and reprint.
As we move forward into more 3D printing use, I predict we're going to find that virtual inventories become one of the bigger benefits of 3D printing across the board.
You can download this replacement fan remote cover from Thingiverse.
Is there a part you need a replacement for?
Let me know what you think you might build in the TalkBacks below.
